content:
# Mark Chapter 6 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), folios 34v–35r (PDF pages 68–69) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/greek-NT/MRK_006_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-NT/MRK_006_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Witness:** Codex Vaticanus (B, 03) — consulted via [DigiVatLib](https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209)

---

¹ Jesus left that area and went back to his hometown, with his disciples following.

² When the Sabbath came, he began teaching in the synagogue. Many who heard him were stunned. "Where did he get all this?" they asked. "What kind of wisdom has he been given? And how does he perform such remarkable miracles?

³ Isn't he just the carpenter? Mary's son? Brother of James, Joses, Judas, and Simon? Don't his sisters live right here among us?" And they refused to believe in him.

⁴ Jesus told them, "A prophet is honored everywhere except in his own hometown, among his own relatives, and in his own house."

⁵ He couldn't do any miracles there—except lay his hands on a few sick people and heal them.

⁶ Their unbelief amazed him. So he moved on, teaching from village to village.

⁷ He called the twelve together and began sending them out in pairs, giving them authority over evil spirits.

⁸ His instructions were simple: take nothing for the road except a walking stick—no food, no travel bag, no money.

⁹ Wear sandals, but don't pack an extra shirt.

¹⁰ He told them, "When you enter a house, stay there until you leave that town.

¹¹ If a place refuses to welcome you or listen to you, leave and shake the dust off your feet as a warning to them."

¹² They went out and called people to turn from their sins.

¹³ They drove out many demons and healed many sick people by anointing them with oil.

¹⁴ King Herod caught wind of all this—Jesus's reputation was spreading everywhere. Some people were saying, "John the Baptist has come back from the dead! That's why he has such miraculous powers."

¹⁵ Others claimed, "He's Elijah." Still others said, "He's a prophet, like the great prophets of long ago."

¹⁶ But when Herod heard about it, he said, "It's John—the one I beheaded. He's come back from the dead."

¹⁷ Here's what had happened: Herod himself had ordered John arrested, chained, and thrown in prison. He did it because of Herodias—his brother Philip's wife—whom Herod had married.

¹⁸ John had been telling Herod bluntly, "It is against God's law for you to marry your brother's wife."

¹⁹ Herodias nursed a bitter grudge against John and wanted him dead, but she couldn't arrange it,

²⁰ because Herod was afraid of John. He knew John was a righteous and holy man, and he protected him. Listening to John disturbed Herod deeply, and yet he kept wanting to hear more.

²¹ Herodias finally got her chance. On Herod's birthday, he threw a banquet for his senior officials, military commanders, and the leading citizens of Galilee.

²² When Herodias's daughter came in and danced, she captivated Herod and all his guests. "Ask me for anything you want," the king told the girl, "and I'll give it to you."

²³ He even made an oath: "Whatever you ask, I'll give you—up to half my kingdom."

²⁴ She slipped out and asked her mother, "What should I request?" Her mother answered instantly: "The head of John the Baptist."

²⁵ The girl rushed back to the king and said, "I want you to give me right now, on a platter, the head of John the Baptist."

²⁶ The king was horrified. But because of his oaths and his dinner guests, he couldn't bring himself to refuse her.

²⁷ So immediately the king dispatched an executioner with orders to bring John's head. The soldier went to the prison and beheaded John,

²⁸ brought his head on a platter and presented it to the girl, and the girl handed it to her mother.

²⁹ When John's disciples heard what had happened, they came, took his body, and laid it in a tomb.

³⁰ The apostles regrouped around Jesus and reported everything they had done and taught.

³¹ He said to them, "Come away with me to a quiet place. You need to rest." The traffic of people coming and going was so heavy they didn't even have time to eat.

³² So they slipped away by boat to a remote spot.

³³ But people saw them leaving and figured out where they were headed. They ran on foot from every nearby town and arrived before them.

³⁴ When Jesus stepped ashore and saw the vast crowd, his heart went out to them—they were like sheep with no shepherd. So he began teaching them at length.

³⁵ By late afternoon, his disciples came to him: "This is a remote place and it's getting late.

³⁶ Send the crowd away so they can go to the farms and villages nearby and buy themselves something to eat."

³⁷ But Jesus said, "You feed them." They stared at him: "Are you serious? It would take almost a year's wages to buy enough bread for this crowd!"

³⁸ "How many loaves do you have?" he asked. "Go check." They came back: "Five—and two fish."

³⁹ He told everyone to sit down in groups on the green grass.

⁴⁰ They settled in orderly groups of fifty and a hundred.

⁴¹ Then Jesus took the five loaves and two fish, looked up to heaven, gave thanks, and broke the bread. He kept handing it to his disciples to distribute. He also divided the two fish among everyone.

⁴² They all ate until they were full.

⁴³ Afterward, the disciples picked up twelve baskets overflowing with leftover bread and fish.

⁴⁴ Five thousand men had eaten.

⁴⁵ Right away Jesus insisted that his disciples get into the boat and head across to Bethsaida while he sent the crowd home.

⁴⁶ After saying goodbye, he went up into the hills to pray.

⁴⁷ By nightfall, the boat was far out on the lake, and Jesus was alone on shore.

⁴⁸ He could see them struggling against the wind, straining hard at the oars. Sometime between three and six in the morning, he came toward them, walking on the lake. He was about to walk right past them,

⁴⁹ but when they saw him walking on the water, they thought he was a ghost and screamed in terror.

⁵⁰ They all saw him and panicked. But immediately he called out, "Courage! It's me. Don't be afraid."

⁵¹ He climbed into the boat with them, and the wind died down. They were completely beside themselves—

⁵² because they still hadn't grasped the meaning of the loaves. Their minds were closed.

⁵³ They crossed the lake and landed at Gennesaret, where they tied up the boat.

⁵⁴ The instant they stepped ashore, people recognized Jesus.

⁵⁵ They raced through the whole area, carrying sick people on mats to wherever they heard he was.

⁵⁶ Everywhere he went—villages, towns, farms—people laid their sick in the marketplaces and begged him to let them touch even the edge of his robe. And everyone who touched it was healed.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

1. **"Just the carpenter" (v. 3):** The contempt is unmistakable. They know him as a manual laborer. In the ancient world, a τέκτων was a woodworker or builder—a skilled trade, but decidedly lower-class. The offense is: how can wisdom and power come from someone we watched grow up?

2. **"He couldn't do any miracles there" (v. 5):** Mark's boldness here is breathtaking. Jesus's power is genuinely constrained by unbelief. Mark does not soften this as Matthew does ("he did not do many" becomes "he could not do any"). I preserve Mark's stark claim.

3. **"Their unbelief amazed him" (v. 6):** The verb θαυμάζω—the same word used when crowds are "amazed" by Jesus—is now turned around. Jesus is the one amazed. It's a profoundly human moment.

4. **The death of John (vv. 14-29):** Mark tells this as a dark, cinematic flashback. The parallel with Jesus's own fate is impossible to miss—a righteous man, arrested, executed at the behest of those in power, his body taken by disciples and placed in a tomb.

5. **"Two hundred denarii" (v. 37):** A denarius was a day's wage for a laborer. Two hundred denarii = roughly eight months' wages. I rendered it as "almost a year's wages" to convey the economic impossibility the disciples are pointing out.

6. **"He was about to walk right past them" (v. 48):** This strange detail echoes Exodus 33 and 1 Kings 19, where God "passes by" in theophany. Jesus is not ignoring the disciples—he is revealing his divine identity through an OT pattern. Combined with "It's me" (ΕΓΩ ΕΙΜΙ) and walking on the sea (Job 9:8), this is one of the most christologically dense scenes in Mark.

7. **"Their minds were closed" (v. 52):** The Greek says their hearts were "hardened" (πεπωρωμένη)—the same word used for Pharaoh. Mark portrays the disciples not as merely slow learners but as spiritually resistant—which makes their later transformation all the more remarkable.

— PNEUMA, AI Translator
